name: Asanga
dates_notes: Asaṅga is conventionally placed in the fourth century on the basis of the dating of his half-brother Vasubandhu (whose Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya engages the developed Vasubandhu-Yogācāra) and Paramārtha's sixth-century Chinese hagiography. Frauwallner (Geschichte II, pp. 264–268) and Schmithausen (Ālayavijñāna, 1987) accept c. 310–390 CE.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhāviveka's Tarkajvālā chapter 5 refutes the Yogācāra trisvabhāva-and-vijñapti-mātratā position on the grounds that the paratantra reintroduces svabhāva, treating the Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha doctrine as the principal Yogācāra position to be answered.
Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.28–32 mounts the canonical Vedāntic refutation of the Yogācāra position; the developed Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha-and-trisvabhāva apparatus stands within the targeted vijñāna-vāda doctrines.

core_thesis: Asaṅga is the founder of the Yogācāra school. His distinctive contribution is the systematic articulation of *vijñapti-mātratā* (cognition-only) within a *trisvabhāva* (three-natures) framework: every cognition has an *imagined* aspect (*parikalpita-svabhāva*) — the falsely projected subject-object duality; a *dependent* aspect (*paratantra-svabhāva*) — the dependently arisen cognitive flow itself; and a *consummate* aspect (*pariniṣpanna-svabhāva*) — the *paratantra* devoid of the *parikalpita*, which is the realised *tathatā*. The *Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha* is the principal text developing this analysis, with the *ālaya-vijñāna* (storehouse-consciousness) introduced as the substrate that carries karmic *vāsanā*-seeds across the moments of the cognitive flow and grounds the appearance of an enduring perceiver. The position is not a denial of objects in some commonsense sense; it is the claim that what *appears* to consciousness as an external object is itself a transformation (*pariṇāma*) of consciousness, and that no extra-cognitive object is required to explain experience. The *Bodhisattva-Bhūmi* supplies the praxis-frame within which the philosophical analysis sits: ten *bhūmi*s of progressive *prajñā*-development culminating in the realisation of *vijñapti-mātratā*. The Maitreya-attributed *Yogācāra-Bhūmi-Śāstra* — recovered by Asaṅga in the tradition's account — is the encyclopedic foundation on which the school's later systematisation rests.

engaged_works.title: Mahayana-Sangraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Asaṅga; survives in Tibetan and in three Chinese translations (Buddhaśānta, Paramārtha, Xuanzang). The base text is foundational for East Asian Yogācāra (Faxiang).
engaged_works.summary: The principal systematic Yogācāra treatise of Asaṅga, structured in ten chapters around the central thesis of *vijñapti-mātratā* developed through the *trisvabhāva* analysis. Chapter one introduces the *ālaya-vijñāna* as the substrate of cognitive flow and as the carrier of *vāsanā*-seeds; chapters two and three develop the *trisvabhāva* (*parikalpita* / *paratantra* / *pariniṣpanna*) as the analytic frame within which the *vijñapti-mātra* thesis is to be understood, with the *paratantra* explicitly characterised as both the locus of the *parikalpita* deception and the locus of the *pariniṣpanna* realisation. The remaining chapters develop the bodhisattva-praxis appropriate to the doctrine: the six *pāramitā*s, the ten *bhūmi*s, the three trainings (*śīla*, *samādhi*, *prajñā*), and the resulting *buddhatva*. The text is the principal Yogācāra source for the Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha-line transmission and the immediate target of Bhāviveka's anti-Yogācāra polemic.

engaged_works.title: Abhidharma-Samuccaya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; survives in Sanskrit (Pradhan 1950 edition reconstructs from Tibetan and Chinese with later Sanskrit fragments) and in Tibetan and Chinese transmission.
engaged_works.summary: Asaṅga's Yogācāra-aligned *abhidharma* compendium, structured around the standard *abhidharma* topics — *skandha*, *dhātu*, *āyatana*, the four noble truths, the *bodhipākṣika-dharma*s — but rewritten from the Mahāyāna and *vijñapti-mātratā* standpoint. The text supplies the bridge between Sarvāstivāda *abhidharma* (which the Vasubandhu of the *Kośa* still represents) and the Yogācāra *abhidharma* that the school requires for its own scholastic instruction. The treatment of *vijñāna* in particular is the formal locus where the *ālaya-vijñāna* doctrine enters the *abhidharma* category-system and is given systematic placement among the eight consciousnesses (six sense-consciousnesses, *manas* as the seventh, *ālaya* as the eighth).

engaged_works.title: Bodhisattva-Bhumi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Asaṅga as one of the principal sections of the larger Yogācāra-Bhūmi-Śāstra. Sanskrit text edited by Wogihara 1930–1936.
engaged_works.summary: The systematic treatise on bodhisattva-praxis structured around the ten *bhūmi*s, with extended treatments of the bodhisattva's *gotra* (lineage-disposition), the cultivation of the four *brahma-vihāra*s and six *pāramitā*s, the ethics of the bodhisattva (*śīla-paṭala*), and the realisation of *tathatā* through *vijñapti-mātra* analysis. The text supplies the praxis-frame within which the *Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha*'s philosophical analysis is to be implemented; together with the *Yogācāra-Bhūmi-Śāstra* to which it belongs, it is the encyclopedic foundation of Yogācāra as an integrated philosophy-and-praxis system.

engaged_works.title: Yogacara-Bhumi-Shastra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed in Tibet to Maitreya (with Asaṅga as recipient-redactor) and in China to Asaṅga himself. Modern scholarship treats the text as the work of multiple hands within the early Yogācāra school, redacted by Asaṅga.

ascription_evidence.description: Schmithausen 1987 treats it as a multi-stratum compilation; the Bodhisattva-Bhūmi section is securely Asaṅga's.

engaged_works.summary: The encyclopedic foundation-text of Yogācāra in seventeen *bhūmi*s, of which the *Bodhisattva-Bhūmi* is one. The text supplies the systematic exposition of the entire Buddhist path from a Yogācāra standpoint: the analysis of *citta* and *caitta*, the path's progression through *bhāvanā* and *prajñā*, the integration of *abhidharma* analysis with *vijñapti-mātra* theory, and the final realisation of *tathatā*. The *Vastusaṅgrahaṇī* and the *Vinaya-Saṃgrahaṇī* sections embed the philosophy in scriptural-exegetical and disciplinary frames. The text is the principal source for the Yogācāra-Bhūmi-line transmission distinct from the *Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha*-line, and is the basis on which both Asaṅga's and Vasubandhu's later systematisations build.

reception_notes: Asaṅga's Yogācāra is engaged in Indian Vedāntic discourse principally through the *vijñāna-vāda* refutation at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.28–32 and through Yāmuna's *Saṃvit-Siddhi*. The *trisvabhāva* analysis is rarely engaged by name in Vedāntic sources, but the *vijñapti-mātratā* thesis (especially in its Vasubandhu-formulation) is the standard Buddhist position the Vedāntic *anumāna*-realists refute when defending external-object realism.
